title: Bures geodesics for non-faithful states and quantum speed limit
abstract: The quantum speed limit establishes a bound on the minimal time required for a quantum system to evolve from a given initial state to a final state. When the mean energy variance is fixed this limitation is captured by the Mandelstam--Tamm bound. The fastest quantum evolution saturating this bound follows a geodesic arc connecting the two states. Such geodesics in the manifold of quantum states are explicitly known when the states are pure (Fubini-Study geodesics) and when they are mixed and given by faithful density matrices (Bures geodesics). In this article we obtain the explicit form of the Bures geodesic arcs joining two non-faithful density matrices, which may have different ranks. For pure states one recovers the Fubini-Study geodesics. A necessary and sufficient condition for the uniqueness of the shortest geodesic arc is given. When the condition is not fulfilled there are infinitely many such arcs, all having length equal to the arccos Bures distance between the two states, in analogy with the arcs of great circles connecting the two poles of a sphere. We discuss the implications of our results for the quantum speed limit.
summary: This paper rigorously determines the geodesic arcs connecting non-faithful quantum states in the space of density matrices using the Bures metric. It establishes necessary and sufficient conditions for the uniqueness of these shortest paths by analyzing the overlap between state supports and kernels. The results have direct implications for understanding the minimum time required for quantum evolution, thus refining the Quantum Speed Limit.
main_problem: The central problem is determining the explicit mathematical form of Bures geodesic arcs connecting two non-faithful quantum states, which relates directly to establishing the Quantum Speed Limit (QSL).
main_result: The authors provide the general framework for these geodesics, showing that while a unique shortest path often exists under specific conditions related to state supports/kernels, infinitely many optimal paths can exist otherwise.
method: The work employs geometric methods on the manifold of density matrices (Bures metric), utilizing techniques like regularization and subspace restriction to analyze boundary behavior for non-invertible states.
model_or_system: The system is general quantum state evolution described by density operators ($ho$) in a finite-dimensional Hilbert space $H$. The analysis focuses on the geometry of this state space, particularly when states are mixed (non-faithful).
key_observables: Bures distance ($d_B$), Fidelity ($F$), Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), and the rank of the geodesic path.
important_parameters: The dimension of the Hilbert space ($n$), the ranks of the initial/final states ($r_1, r_2$), and regularization parameters ($\epsilon$).
main_assumptions: The analysis often relies on approximating boundary geodesics by taking limits from faithful (invertible) states or restricting the path to subspaces spanned by the supports of the end-states.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate geodesic arcs connecting qubit states, showing differences between unique shortest paths and cases with infinitely many optimal arcs when states are orthogonal. Tables summarize properties based on state ranks for specific dimensions like qutrits and qubits.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from the known pure-state (Fubini-Study) case to general mixed states, developing techniques involving regularization and subspace analysis to handle non-faithful states ($	ext{rank} < n$). It culminates in explicit conditions for geodesic uniqueness based on kernel/support intersections.
why_interesting: This work provides rigorous geometric tools to analyze the fundamental limits of quantum evolution (QSL) by characterizing the shortest paths between arbitrary mixed states, which is crucial for understanding decoherence and process characterization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a) Geodesic arcs connecting two faithful non-commuting qubit states, represented on the hypersphere S3

FIG. 2: Geodesic arcs joining the perturbed states ρ1(ε1) and ρ2(ε2) of two non-faithful states ρ1 and ρ2 in the regularization method (plain red and blue curves). The regularization paths εi 7→ρi(εi), i = 1, 2, are shown in dashed lines.

FIG. 3: Spectrum of γ1→2 g (τ) vs time τ for |z| = 0.5 and |z| = 1.0, showing that for all τ ∈(0, θ), γ1→2 g (τ) has rank 3 in the first case and rank 2 in the second case. The vertical dashed line indicates t = θ and arg(z) = π.

FIG. 4: Complex-plane representation of the limit limε→0 z(cm,n) 21 (ε, ε) for the regularization curves (B2) with r = rm and ϕ = ϕn as in (B3). The colored circles and gray rays indicate resp. the radial index m and phase index n. The distribution of points shows that the limiting values fill the closed unit disk. The value r = 1 leads to Fubini geodesics, given by (44) with |z21| = 1 and arbitrary phases ϕ; the values 0 < r < 1 lead to elliptic rank-2 geodesics, and r = 0 corresponds to the rank-2 straight diameter geodesic.
